notes: I played this game for five hours, and I can certainly say that it runs fine. Keyword being fine, the framerate isn't all that good. When playing on max settings, my framerate was ranging from 45 to 60. On the smallest graphical settings however, I had 100+ for the most part; considering my PC specs, that isn't super great, but I don't have Windows to compare it to, so take my framerate issue with a pinch of salt. That being said, there was no input lag or issues whatsoever. The only graphical issue I found was the screen flickering white every-now-and-then. Apron further inspection, it really isn't the screen flickering one color, it's like a huge bright white sponge consumes the world (You can see holes where models can be seen). This ONLY happens to me when it's raining in the game, and that isn't often. For the most part on rainy maps, it's playable... Some textures does flicker white tho, but this rarely happens to me. Another issue that keeps happening is the game-music disappearing randomly... Not sure what causes this, and I don't know of this is normal for the game. With all that said, I didn't have to tweak anything to get this game running.

notes:
installed d9vk 0.12 to get better performance than on windows 10/wine's opengl (vega 56 doesn't play nice with ue3 with dx9) to install d9vk first head to the releases page https://github.com/Joshua-Ashton/d9vk/releases extract the tarball, then open a terminal in the extracted folder. Run these two commands to install d9vk into your games wine prefix "export WINEPREFIX=/home/USERNAMEHERELALALALALALA/.local/share/Steam/steamapps/compatdata/253230/pfx" enter "./setup_dxvk.sh install" after updates you will need to reapply d9vk as the prefix will be overwritten.

On another positive note the game no longer has graphical glitches on the raining mafia town levels.

title: A Hat in Time

notes: Tried the tweak (installing d9vk) below, and it is so much better than playing it out of the box on my GT 1030. Highly recommend to install d9vk.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: ***tl;dr use D9VK and disable esync for near-native performance with max settings. No more crashes between levels, though performance isn't as good as using galliumnine. Steam Controller works perfect.*** Full report: Disable esync (right click > Properties > Set Launch Options > "PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1 %command%") as this fixes stuttering and slightly extended load times, and install D9VK for a massive performance bump. You can do this by either building Proton with D9VK yourself, or you can follow these instructions to install it to a pre-existing Proton prefix: Launch A Hat in Time at least once after installing if you haven't already, as Steam needs to create the Proton prefix. Go to this link (https://github.com/Joshua-Ashton/d9vk/releases) and download the latest package. At the time of writing, it's "0.12". Extract it anywhere, then set your WINE prefix to wherever the prefix for A Hat in Time is by running "export WINEPREFIX=/path/to/library/folder/steamapps/compatdata/253230/". In the same Bash session (i.e. same Terminal window), run "./setup_dxvk.sh install". This should take all of about 30 seconds. Again, run with esync disabled and you're good to go! At max settings (excluding motion blur, which I disabled for personal preference) and fps capped at 60 I got equivalent if not *better* performance to Windows - That is to say, occasional stutter when generating new effects i.e. using the Scooter or using the Witches Hat bomb thingy. With the framerate unlocked I stayed around 70-90fps. At the end of some time rifts, when you're hitting the rift with your Umbrella or whatever, framerate dips to 27-34fps. D9VK seems to fix the crashing every other level, which means you don't have to worry about game crashes. I didn't test any online features or anything beyond Mafia Town and Dead Bird Studio. Galliumnine did give about 120-140fps (Massively beyond native performance) but not only does this break the Steam overlay - making the Steam Controller unusable - but the load times are exceptionally long; about 2-4 minutes per level and 5 minutes for the game to start while running on a Samsung SSD. The game also crashed every other level, meaning you could only play one level at a time. The same issue is present with standard DXVK/WINE OpenGL. With D9VK, while CPU usage is a bit higher the experience is overall much better. A seamless experience with D9VK.

title: A Hat in Time

notes: Install D9VK and install Esync to fix performance (see other reports on how to install D9VK, very quick and easy). Played through the entire base game (no DLC levels) and tried out one Death Wish level. In my 20 hours of playing, the game crashed about 7 times. 4 of those were on a loading screen, 2 were mid-game (one at the end of Chapter 3 Act 1, and one during the first Death Wish level) and one was in Chapter 2 Act 1 - This crash is an interesting one because it's the only consistent one. If you put on the sunglasses in Dead Bird Studio, the game will freeze after about 4 seconds. This not only crashes the game but also the entire system. You need to reboot after this crash. I don't know if this happens when DXVK or if it's a problem with Mesa or what, but I'm sure with updates to both it'll get fixed. Modded levels work fine, as do asset swap mods, custom hats, etc. Full workshop support in my testing. The game usually hovers around 70-90fps and has some stuttering when loading new effects, for example the explosion with the witch hat. Very very minor details. Perfectly playable and extremely enjoyable. If it weren't for the crashes I'd forget that the game wasn't native.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Install D9VK and disable esync, detailed in previous reports, for almost perfect performance. Playing a Chapter for the first time might take about 45 seconds to load, but subsequent loads will be about 15-20 seconds (depending on stage  - Alpine Skyline and Nyakuza Metro take a little bit longer than the others). Played through a Challenge Road with no issues (just minor stutter) and the entire Artic Cruise DLC stage. I only experienced two crashes in the 10 or so hours it took to complete. I also played a couple of Death Wish levels and they worked fine - Including the one that initially crashed. Speaking of crashes, the lockup encountered in Dead Bird Studio with the sunglasses seems to now be fixed! That, combined with the reduction in crashes, means really the only thing keeping this game from having a Platinum rating is having to install D9VK. I played about 5 minutes of Nyakuza Metro and performance is decent. Hovers around the 45-60fps mark, compared to the rest of the game being 70-90fps. However I am playing the game at max settings, and I haven't tested this specific level on Windows. Don't let this deter you, as without the FPS counter it's not all that noticeable - and even with the counter the game is still fully playable. Again, if not for needing performance tweaks then the game would have a Platinum rating. DXVK on it's own gives such low performance for the game, hence why I recommend D9VK and subsequently disabling esync. I have yet to try the online multiplayer, though I plan to test it out soon. Most likely after completing and reporting back on the performance of the Nyakuza Metro chapter. A very small note: After installing the Challenge Road DLC stages and restarting the game ( only after restarting and not while playing), the game was using about 80% CPU. Exiting to the main menu and resuming the game fixed this issue. RAM usage also skyrocketed. It hit a peak of 6GB, though currently it's at 4.5GB. I only noticed this after playing the Nyakuza Metro chapter, so I can't say for sure if it's the mods or stage or something else entirely. I'll look into it some more. Some other misc points: No graphical glitches with D9VK, game runs at max with 70-90fps in most cases, mods work, controllers work (tested with Steam Controller), Steam API works (provided you don't use galliumnine), achievements work, all DLC works, game can be completed from start-to-finish. Overall, a very nice experience and thanks to Proton I'm getting addicted to the game all over again.
title: A Hat in Time

notes:
I've played this game for 80+ hours now and have tested this game with the latest proton version for over 5 hours, and I can safely say this game just runs out of the box without any tweaks.
Preformance isn't super great however, in order to test how well this game runs, go to settings -> game settings and enable "Developer Console". Then press TAB while in game and type "stat fps" - this will show you how many frames per second you get while playing.

Something noteworthy is that I disabled vsync and the framerate limiter in the settings as well.


When running this game at "Very High Quality" (the highest) the FPS ranges from 30 to 120, but 80% of the time hangs out at around 60. If you play on a map where it rains however, the FPS drops sigificantly and starts to hang out around 20-45.

When playing on "Standard" (middle settings) the FPS ranges from 45 to 80, 80% of the time it's at 60. When playing on a map that rains, it drops down to 35-60 and usually is at 45.

When running this game at "Preformance" (the lowest) the FPS ranges from 80 to 150 and 80% of the time is at 110. When it rains it's at 55 to 110, usually at 60.


There are some graphcal issues while playing, some textures are completely black. This seems to happen to a specific prop though, a rock of some sort, and it doesn't impact gameplay. This prop doesn't appear often either, so this issue is minimal.

What will impact your gameplay however, is a white flickering screen.
On maps where it rains, the screen will sometimes flicker completely white, as if you were inside a white sponge (you will sometimes see sphere-holes where there isn't any white, but normal graphics). This issue happens no matter which graphical settings you have.

There is also a similar bug where some textures become white when looked at from an angle, this also happens no matter what graphical settings you have. At least this doesn't flicker and this is pretty rare and only impacts some maps, this also has minimal impacts on gameplay.

Because of this, I feel inclined to add an EPILEPSY WARNING for maps that have rain in them.

And lastly, some workshop maps do make the screen flicker white (as described above), even though there is no rain in them. This only happens on some workshop maps and is rare as well, though I think it's important to mention this.


The rest of the game works perfectly however, multiplayer works (A DLC is required for this), DLCs work and workshop maps and mods works.
I do have to stress though: EPILEPSY WARNING!
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Install D9VK, disable esync, and you're good to go. D9VK fixes the rain flashing glitches, however performance still takes a hit (this happens on Windows also though). The Online Party multiplayer works, and cutscenes work - It seems it was an update to the game that fixed this. I believe it was sometimes broken on Windows too and the latest game update addressed it. Solid performance, fullscreen, borderless and windowed mode all work, and the Nyakuza Metro DLC chapter - while the worst offender in terms of performance - is fully and comfortably playable. Updates to DXVK/D9VK should only improve how this game runs.

notes: Install d9vk for windows level performance. To do that, download the latest release from the github (https://github.com/Joshua-Ashton/d9vk/releases) and extract it. Then copy d9vk-xxx/x64/d3d9.dll into ~/.local/share/Steam/steamapps/common/HatinTime/Binaries/Win64/ . Then set the launch options to "WINEDLLOVERRIDES="d3d9=n" %command%". Completed 100% run without dlc. Only crashes when loading the final credits scene.

notes:
Used to have swinging tree textures and other missing textures (both that turned black and others just not appearing), the rain also had a weird effect which made the game almost unplayable. 

Using d9vk removed all these issues but made the game a lot slower 50% drop.
Disabling Esync brought the performance up higher than it was before.
Launch options: "WINEDLLOVERRIDES="d3d9=n" PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1 %command%"
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Install D9VK (either through downloading it and pasting the DLL into the Hat in Time folder, or using the installer script that comes with D9VK), disable esync (PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1) and you'll get native performance. I get slightly better performance with D9VK, but it's marginal. Played with everything at max, including motion blur. Of course vsync is off as is recommended for pretty much all games even on Windows, and framerate locked to 60fps for my own preference. Some minor dips to 58 during gameplay, Nyakuza Metro brings the framerate down to about 40fps, loading screens are about 28fps, and the game has pretty much no crashes. Sometimes trying to load some Subcon Forest levels will crash the game but it's very rare. Tested two challenge roads and they all worked perfect, Death Wish works perfect, I have about 80 mods installed from the workshop and they all work, and the online multiplayer works as well. I've only played with a party of 3 (including myself). Local co-op works, console commands work, everything works. The game is basically running like native and it is a joy to play with Proton.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: It works really well out of the box but it has some minor light glitch in the first chapter and some fps drop in the Nyakuza metro DLC chapter which can be annoying during platforming
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Requires D9VK (https://github.com/Joshua-Ashton/d9vk) to run well. Simply download the latest release, extract it, copy (or symlink) <EXTRACT_PATH>/x64/d3d9.dll into <STEAM_LIBRARY>/steamapps/common/HatinTime/Binaries/Win64/ and add the following as launch options: «WINEDLLOVERRIDES="d3d9=n" PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1 %command%» (without «»). Some stuttering happens when shaders are being built, but runs at a stable 60fps once the shader cache is filled.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: You have to use D9VK or you will have terrible performance and bad graphical glitches. Follow the instructions at https://github.com/Joshua-Ashton/d9vk to install D9VK into your prefix. The game stutters a bit as shaders are being compiled but otherwise runs great, no need to disable esync either if your open file limit is set properly. 'Seal the Deal' and 'Nyakuza Metro' DLCs both work as well.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Played through the entire main game (100% timepieces), as well as put a few hours into both of the DLCs. The main game runs fairly well, although there is freezing on entering the levels, and the levels with rain have graphical glitches that can render the game unplayable. The metro DLC runs so slowly as to be unplayable, the death wish DLC suffers from leaning heavily on a rain-based level leading to sometimes becoming a white screen, and the arctic cruise DLC seems to run fine.

notes: With the proton 4.11-1 just enable d9vk on settings and game runs perfectly. Super smooth, great performance, perfect graphics, sound and controls.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Use these as the launch arguments to get a basically flawless experience: "PROTON_FORCE_LARGE_ADDRESS_AWARE=1 PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 %command% -skipintro" (Game runs out of virtual memory if large address aware is not set, even though it's a 64bit game)
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Running with D9VK brought on smooth 60FPS play at mostly-default graphics settings. Be aware that the intial performance will just jumpy because of shader caching, but once most shaders are out of the way, you'll play just fine.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Greatly improved performance when using D9VK (PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 %command%).
title: A Hat in Time

notes:
The game works fine most of the time, with minor graphical issues (as others have stated, such as the rain in "She Came From Outer Space"); however, I sometimes get the error stating that I ran out of memory (which never happened to me on Windows 10, so its not my hardware).
I think it might be the lack of support of Vulkan on Bumblebee driver on laptop (https://github.com/felixdoerre/primus_vk), like maybe the memory is not cleared correctly so it just stacks up??
To run steam, I use this command:
"vblank_mode=0 LD_PRELOAD='/usr/$LIB/libstdc++.so.6 /usr/$LIB/libgcc_s.so.1 /usr/$LIB/libxcb.so.1' ENABLE_PRIMUS_LAYER=1 optirun -b primus /usr/bin/steam %U"
Launch options for the game:
"PROTON_FORCE_LARGE_ADDRESS_AWARE=1 PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 %command% -skipintro"
title: A Hat in Time

gpu: NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1060 with Max-Q Design
notes: 4.11 using D9VK and feral gamemode runs perfectly at max settings. Without gamemode, some graphic stutter is still present with all graphics maxed out. Mafia Town's rain issue and Dead Bird Studio's white wall issue are both fixed. The issue requiring wiping the settings file on every launch to prevent an error is also fixed. Launch options for me are "PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 gamemoderun %command%".
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Runs great when using D9VK (available in Proton 4.11-1). Add the following (inside «») to launch options: «PROTON_FORCE_LARGE_ADDRESS_AWARE=1 PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 %command%». The former seems to have fixed a crash I had when loading one of the levels in Subcon Forest and the latter enables D9VK. Some stuttering when loading shaders, but otherwise great.
title: A Hat in Time

notes:
*D9VK - Decreased stuttering slightly and fixed a random system crash when entering the Kitchen.
*Disabling Esync & Enabling LAA - Helped with shader loading and made loading screens smoother. 
*Feral Gamemode - Decreased stuttering significantly.
title: A Hat in Time

notes:
Online Party seems to crash the game when the party is blank (didn't try with a party that has players).
Mod Manager is broken under Proton due to .net support (will launch under wine with .net but not under Proton either) and therefore couldn't try the editor.
Performance is acceptable and is improved by using D9VK.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Using the builtin Proton tweaks, the game runs more than comfortably. If you install the third-party tool developed by Feral Interactive (they handle a lot of native-Linux and macOS game ports) called Feral GameMode (available in the AUR or here: https://github.com/FeralInteractive/gamemode) you can get that extra little bit of smooth performance. Online works, mods work (I have a ton installed), game may stutter a little bit the first time you load it up but I can run at max settings INCLUDING VSYNC at a full solid 60fps. I didn't have any of the online crashes mentioned in previous reports. Use the following launch options, leaving out "gamemoderun" if you do not intend to use the tool, to get pretty much native performance: "PROTON_NO_ESYNC=1 PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 PROTON_LARGE_ADDRESS PROTON_FORCE_LARGE_ADDRESS_AWARE=1gamemoderun %command%"
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Runs perfectly with Proton, no tweaks required
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Disable esync, using the Large Address Aware flag, use D9VK, and you can run the game at max settings. Use Feral GameMode optionally for even better performance. I can run the game, including mod levels, with all settings at max without dipping below 60fps. Capping the framerate can help reduce stutter, but this goes for all games and not just A Hat in Time, and not even just Proton games. It basically runs like native - And I play with a ton of mods.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Needed "PROTON_USE_D9VK=1 %command" to get good framerate.
title: A Hat in Time

notes: Like Overwatch, this game currently makes extensive use of the DXVK/D9VK state cache and will try to compile on the fly. LLVM is slower than the Nvidia compiler while ACO is pretty much on par performance with the Nvidia compiler, albeit less stable right now. Expect brief game lockups (Don't worry if it seems frozen, it's not frozen, it's just compiling) as LLVM and D9VK build shaders, with wait times for shader compilation being the longest on the biggest levels.
title: A Hat in Time
